content.text: read @CLAUDE.md

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project Overview

**AYA (And You Are?)** is a Python authentication/authorization library for web frameworks. It is structured as a monorepo with two packages:
- **`aya-dash/`**: Plotly Dash + Flask integration (mature, production-ready)
- **`aya-fastapi/`**: FastAPI integration (stub, not yet implemented)

The root `pyproject.toml` defines the `aya` metapackage with optional extras `aya[dash]` and `aya[fastapi]`.

**Do not touch `aya-fastapi/`** — it is a stub and out of scope.

## Development Commands

This project uses `uv` as the package manager and `hatchling` as the build backend.

```bash
# Install dependencies for aya-dash
cd aya-dash && uv sync

# Lint and format (run from either package directory)
uv run ruff check .
uv run ruff format .
```

No test suite is currently configured.

## Code Style

- **Linter/Formatter**: `ruff`
- **Line length**: 120 characters
- **Import sorting**: enabled (`I` ruleset)

## Architecture

### aya-dash

The package provides OIDC/OAuth2 authentication for Plotly Dash apps via four files:

**`authentication.py`** — Core auth logic:
- `IdPSettings` — `Protocol` that settings classes must satisfy (`client_id`, `client_secret`, `scope`, `metadata_url`, `jwks_uri`, `issuer`, `end_session_fallback`).
- `configure_authentication(app, settings, home_page="/", secure=False, authorization_validators=None)` — main entry point. Registers `/login`, `/authorized`, `/logout` Flask routes and two `before_request` hooks (`expire_session`, `check_auth`). No `dash_auth.Auth` subclass — auth is pure Flask middleware.
- `decode_jwt(token, jwks_uri, issuer)` — verifies JWT signature via JWKS and validates `iss` + expiry. Returns claims dict or `{}` on failure. Used by decorators on every protected request.
- `decode_jwt_without_verification(token)` — base64-decodes JWT payload without signature check. Used only by `get_user_name()` for display purposes after login.
- `get_user_name()` — reads `name` / `preferred_username` from session `access_token`.

**`settings.py`** — Pydantic Settings:
- `KeycloakSettings` — reads Keycloak config from env. Computes `metadata_url`, `jwks_uri`, `issuer`, `end_session_fallback` from `KEYCLOAK_URL` + `REALM`. `client_secret` is `SecretStr`.
- `EntraSettings` — reads Microsoft Entra ID config from env (`AZURE_TENANT_ID`). Computes equivalent properties against `login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant_id}`. Both implement `IdPSettings`.

**`validators.py`** — JWT claim validator factories. All return `Callable[[dict], bool]`:
- `validator_for_keycloak_realm_role_in(roles)` — checks `realm_access.roles`.
- `validator_for_keycloak_client_role_in(client_id, roles)` — checks `resource_access.<client_id>.roles`.
- `validator_for_keycloak_group_in(groups)` — checks `groups` claim.
- `validator_for_entra_role_in(roles)` — checks `roles` claim (Entra ID app roles).
- `_build_validator(client_id, roles, groups)` — internal; combines validators with **OR logic** (user must satisfy roles OR groups). Raises if neither given.

**`decorators.py`** — Dash/Flask protection helpers. All call `decode_jwt` (with verification) on each invocation:
- `require_role(roles, groups, client_id, on_denied)` — Flask route decorator. Aborts 403 on failure.
- `protect_layout(layout, roles, groups, client_id, on_denied)` — wraps static layout into callable for `dash.register_page`. Guards `has_request_context()` to avoid startup crash.
- `protect_component(component, roles, groups, client_id, on_denied)` — call inside function layout; returns `None` (omits component) if unauthorized.
- `protect_callback(roles, groups, client_id, on_denied)` — Dash callback decorator; raises `PreventUpdate` if unauthorized.

### Auth Flow

1. `before_request` → `expire_session` clears session if `exp` is past.
2. `before_request` → `check_auth` skips `/login`, `/authorized`, `/logout`, `/assets`, `/_favicon`. For `/_dash*` paths aborts 401; for all others redirects to `/login`.
3. `/login` → `oauth.idp.authorize_redirect()` → IdP.
4. IdP returns to `/authorized` → token exchanged, `decode_jwt` verifies signature + issuer, `authorization_validators` run; on pass, stores `access_token` + `id_token` + `exp` in session.
5. `/logout` → clears session, redirects to IdP `end_session_endpoint` with `id_token_hint`.

### Design Principles

- **Two IdP paths** — `KeycloakSettings` for Keycloak (which can federate Azure AD/LDAP), `EntraSettings` for direct Entra ID integration.
- **JWT verified on every protected request** — decorators call `decode_jwt` (JWKS) not just at login.
- **OR logic for role/group** — `_build_validator` passes if user satisfies roles OR groups (not both).
- **No HTML rendering** — AYA handles auth backend only; consumers build their own login/error UI.
- **`client_id` defaults from env** — decorators read `OIDC_CLIENT_ID` / `CLIENT_ID` via `KeycloakSettings` if not passed explicitly.

### Environment Variables

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `KEYCLOAK_URL` | Keycloak base URL (default: `https://auth.ltplabs.com`) |
| `REALM` | Keycloak realm name |
| `OIDC_CLIENT_ID` / `CLIENT_ID` | OAuth client ID |
| `OIDC_CLIENT_SECRET` / `CLIENT_SECRET` | OAuth client secret |
| `OIDC_SCOPE` / `SCOPE` | OAuth scopes (Keycloak default: `openid`; Entra default: `openid email profile`) |
| `AZURE_TENANT_ID` | Microsoft Entra ID tenant ID (`EntraSettings` only) |

# aya-react Keycloak OIDC authentication for React apps. Internal LTPLabs package. ## Install Add to your project's `.npmrc`: ```ini @ltplabs:registry=https://git.ltplabs.net/api/v4/projects/640/packages/npm/ //git.ltplabs.net/api/v4/projects/640/packages/npm/:_authToken= ``` Then: ```bash npm install @ltplabs/aya-react ``` Get a token from GitLab → User → Preferences → Access Tokens (scope: `read_api`). ## Environment Variables Create a `.env` file in your project root (copy from `.env.example`): ```ini VITE_KEYCLOAK_URL=https://auth.example.com/ VITE_REALM=my-realm VITE_OIDC_CLIENT_ID=my-client ``` > Vite requires the `VITE_` prefix to expose variables to the browser. > The Keycloak client must be **public** (`Client authentication: OFF`) with PKCE (`S256`) enabled — this library never sends a client secret. Wire them into the provider: ```tsx ``` ## Usage ```tsx import { AuthProvider, useAuth, Protected } from "@ltplabs/aya-react"; function App() { const { user, token, logout, hasRole, hasGroup } = useAuth(); return
Hello, {user.name}
; } Access denied

}> ``` ## API ### `` | Prop | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | `url` | `string` | Keycloak server URL | | `realm` | `string` | Keycloak realm | | `clientId` | `string` | Client ID for this app | Renders `null` until Keycloak initialises. Handles token refresh automatically. ### `useAuth()` | Field | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | `user` | `{ name, email, username }` | Parsed from token claims | | `token` | `string` | Raw access token for API calls | | `logout` | `() => void` | Ends session | | `hasRole(role)` | `(string) => boolean` | Checks realm + resource roles, for the client that is being used | | `hasGroup(group)` | `(string) => boolean` | Checks `groups` claim , for the client that is being used| ### `` | Prop | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | `roles` | `string[]` | User must have at least one | | `groups` | `string[]` | User must belong to at least one | | `onDenied` | `ReactNode` | Rendered when access denied (default: `null`) | #### Examples Guard by role — renders nothing if user lacks the role: ```tsx ``` Guard by role with fallback message: ```tsx You do not have access to this page.

}> ``` Guard by group: ```tsx ``` Require role AND group (both must pass): ```tsx ``` Require one of multiple roles (any one is enough): ```tsx ``` ## Demo ```bash cd demo cp .env.example .env # fill in your Keycloak values npm install npm run dev ``` ## Publishing Push a tag matching `react-v*` — CI publishes automatically. ```bash git tag react-v0.1.0 git push origin react-v0.1.0 ```

# Handoff — aya-react

## Goal

Create a React/TypeScript internal npm package (`aya-react`) that wraps Keycloak OIDC authentication — the React equivalent of what `aya-dash` does for Plotly Dash. Distributed via a self-hosted GitLab npm registry.

The package should let app developers drop in auth with minimal wiring:

```tsx
<AuthProvider>
  <App />
</AuthProvider>
```

---

## Decisions Made

### Separate repo (not monorepo with aya-dash)
GitLab Package Registry is project-scoped. One repo per package is the natural fit. Frontend teams consuming this package have no context on the Python side.

### Self-hosted GitLab as npm registry
Packages published to `https://<gitlab-host>/api/v4/projects/<id>/packages/npm/`. Consumers configure `.npmrc` with a deploy token or personal access token.

### Tooling choices
| Concern | Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Build | `tsup` | Outputs ESM + CJS from TS, zero config |
| Auth | `keycloak-js` | Official Keycloak JS adapter, handles PKCE + token refresh |
| Language | TypeScript strict | Consistency with internal frontend standards |

---

## Next Steps

1. **Create GitLab repo** named `aya-react` under the appropriate group.

2. **Scaffold package structure**:
   ```
   aya-react/
   ├── package.json         # name: "@<scope>/aya-react", publishConfig pointing to GitLab registry
   ├── tsconfig.json        # strict, target ESNext, moduleResolution bundler
   ├── tsup.config.ts       # entry: src/index.ts, format: [esm, cjs], dts: true
   └── src/
       ├── index.ts         # re-exports everything
       ├── AuthProvider.tsx  # <AuthProvider>, AuthContext, initialises keycloak-js
       ├── useAuth.ts       # useAuth() → { user, logout, token, hasRole, hasGroup }
       └── Protected.tsx    # <Protected roles={[]} groups={[]}> — renders children or null
   ```

3. **Implement `AuthProvider`** — initialise `Keycloak` from `keycloak-js` with `{ url, realm, clientId }` props. Use `keycloak.init({ onLoad: "login-required" })`. Store instance in context.

4. **Implement `useAuth`** hook — reads from `AuthContext`, exposes:
   - `user: { name, email, username }` — from `keycloak.tokenParsed`
   - `token: string` — raw access token for API calls
   - `logout: () => void` — calls `keycloak.logout()`
   - `hasRole(role: string): boolean` — checks `keycloak.hasRealmRole` and `keycloak.hasResourceRole`
   - `hasGroup(group: string): boolean` — checks `groups` claim in `tokenParsed`

5. **Implement `<Protected>`** — renders children if `hasRole`/`hasGroup` passes, otherwise renders `null` (or `onDenied` prop).

6. **Configure GitLab CI** (`.gitlab-ci.yml`) — job that runs `npm publish` on version tag (`v*`). Use `CI_JOB_TOKEN` for registry auth.

7. **Write `.npmrc`** for local dev pointing to GitLab registry.

8. **Update `odoo.md`** in `aya-dash` repo with React usage example once package is published.

---

## Context / Notes

- `keycloak-js` uses `Keycloak` class (default export). Init is async — wrap in `useEffect` + loading state before rendering children.
- Token refresh: `keycloak.onTokenExpired = () => keycloak.updateToken(30)` — set this up in `AuthProvider` to avoid silent expiry mid-session.
- `keycloak.tokenParsed` contains `realm_access.roles`, `resource_access.<clientId>.roles`, `groups` (if mapper configured in Keycloak). Same claims structure as `aya-dash` validators.
- GitLab registry URL format: `https://<host>/api/v4/projects/<project-id>/packages/npm/` — get the project ID from GitLab → Settings → General.
- `publishConfig` in `package.json` must match the registry URL in `.npmrc` or publish will fail silently to the wrong registry.
- Peer deps: `react >=17`, `react-dom >=17`. Do not bundle React.
- Reference: `aya-dash` repo at `joaobarbosa/feat/add-dash-keycloak-integration` for session structure, validator logic, and `KeycloakSettings` env var names — the claims structure is identical.

default:
  image: node:20

stages:
  - build
  - publish

build:
  stage: build
  script:
    - npm ci
    - npm run build
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - dist/

publish:
  stage: publish
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_TAG =~ /^v/
  script:
    - |
      echo "@ltplabs:registry=${CI_API_V4_URL}/projects/${CI_PROJECT_ID}/packages/npm/" >> .npmrc
      echo "${CI_API_V4_URL#https:}/projects/${CI_PROJECT_ID}/packages/npm/:_authToken=${CI_JOB_TOKEN}" >> .npmrc
    - npm publish

stages:
  - build
  - publish

aya-react:build:
  stage: build
  image: node:20
  rules:
    - changes:
        - aya-react/**/*
  script:
    - cd aya-react
    - npm ci
    - npm run build
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - aya-react/dist/

aya-react:publish:
  stage: publish
  image: node:20
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_TAG =~ /^react-v/
  script:
    - cd aya-react
    - |
      echo "@ltplabs:registry=${CI_API_V4_URL}/projects/${CI_PROJECT_ID}/packages/npm/" >> .npmrc
      echo "${CI_API_V4_URL#https:}/projects/${CI_PROJECT_ID}/packages/npm/:_authToken=${CI_JOB_TOKEN}" >> .npmrc
    - npm publish

aya-dash:publish:
  stage: publish
  image: python:3.11
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_TAG =~ /^dash-v/
  script:
    - pip install uv
    - cd aya-dash
    - uv build
    - >
      uv publish
      --publish-url ${CI_API_V4_URL}/projects/${CI_PROJECT_ID}/packages/pypi
      --username gitlab-ci-token
      --password ${CI_JOB_TOKEN}

# React + TypeScript + Vite

This template provides a minimal setup to get React working in Vite with HMR and some Oxlint rules.

Currently, two official plugins are available:

- [@vitejs/plugin-react](https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-react/blob/main/packages/plugin-react) uses [Oxc](https://oxc.rs)
- [@vitejs/plugin-react-swc](https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-react/blob/main/packages/plugin-react-swc) uses [SWC](https://swc.rs/)

## React Compiler

The React Compiler is not enabled on this template because of its impact on dev & build performances. To add it, see [this documentation](https://react.dev/learn/react-compiler/installation).

## Expanding the Oxlint configuration

If you are developing a production application, we recommend enabling type-aware lint rules by installing `oxlint-tsgolint` and editing `.oxlintrc.json`:

```json
{
  "$schema": "./node_modules/oxlint/configuration_schema.json",
  "plugins": ["react", "typescript", "oxc"],
  "options": {
    "typeAware": true
  },
  "rules": {
    "react/rules-of-hooks": "error",
    "react/only-export-components": ["warn", { "allowConstantExport": true }]
  }
}
```

See the [Oxlint rules documentation](https://oxc.rs/docs/guide/usage/linter/rules) for the full list of rules and categories.

Not fully. It builds/typechecks, and PKCE is mostly implemented, but there are several setup and security gaps before I’d call `aya-react` production-ready. ## Checks run From `aya-react/`: ```bash npm run typecheck npm run build npm pack --dry-run ``` Both typecheck/build pass, but packaging is currently broken. ## Missing / incorrect setup ### 1. Package exports are wrong `tsup` outputs: ```txt dist/index.js # CJS dist/index.mjs # ESM ``` But `package.json` says: ```json "main": "./dist/index.cjs", "module": "./dist/index.js", "exports": { ".": { "import": "./dist/index.js", "require": "./dist/index.cjs" } } ``` Problems: - `dist/index.cjs` does not exist. - ESM import points to `dist/index.js`, which is actually CommonJS. - Consumers may fail to import the package. Should be closer to: ```json "main": "./dist/index.js", "module": "./dist/index.mjs", "types": "./dist/index.d.ts", "exports": { ".": { "types": "./dist/index.d.ts", "import": "./dist/index.mjs", "require": "./dist/index.js" } } ``` ### 2. Demo does not demo the package `aya-react/demo/src/main.tsx` only renders: ```tsx

Hello World

``` So the package is not actually exercised with `AuthProvider`, `useAuth`, or `Protected`. ### 3. `Protected` behavior does not match `aya-dash` `aya-dash` uses OR logic for roles/groups: > user must satisfy roles OR groups Current React implementation uses AND when both are provided: ```ts if (roleOk && groupOk) return children; ``` That may be intentional, but it is not “like aya-dash”. ### 4. `hasRole()` checks roles from all clients Current code: ```ts Object.values(resourceAccess).flatMap(...) ``` This means a role from another Keycloak client can authorize the user in this app. Security-wise, this should probably check: ```ts resource_access[clientId].roles ``` plus realm roles if desired. ## PKCE status PKCE itself is mostly there: - generates `code_verifier` - stores it in `sessionStorage` - sends `code_challenge` - uses `S256` - validates returned `state` - exchanges code without client secret That part is directionally correct for a public SPA client. Keycloak client config still needs: - Client authentication: OFF - Standard flow: ON - PKCE required / S256 - Valid redirect URI matching `window.location.origin` - Web origins configured for the SPA origin - Short refresh/access token lifetimes - Refresh token rotation if available ## Security concerns ### Important: this cannot secure APIs by itself Unlike `aya-dash`, this is browser-side. `Protected` and `hasRole()` only hide UI. They are not authorization enforcement. Any backend/API called with this token must independently validate: - JWT signature - issuer - audience / authorized party - expiry - roles/groups ### Tokens are stored in `sessionStorage` Better than `localStorage`, but still readable by XSS. The refresh token is the biggest risk. Options: - best security: BFF/server-side session pattern - acceptable SPA tradeoff: keep tokens in memory only - if using `sessionStorage`: enforce strong CSP, no unsafe inline scripts, short token lifetimes, refresh rotation ### Token parsing is not validation `parseJwt()` only decodes claims. That is fine for display/UI hints, but not security validation. ### Logout is incomplete/old-style Current logout: ```ts /logout?redirect_uri=... ``` Keycloak commonly expects OIDC RP-initiated logout with things like: - `post_logout_redirect_uri` - `id_token_hint` - sometimes `client_id` You currently do not keep the `id_token`, so you cannot send `id_token_hint`. ### No nonce For OIDC login flows, a `nonce` is commonly included and checked against the ID token. Since this code does not request/store/validate the ID token, nonce validation is missing. ### URL handling is fragile This assumes `url` ends with `/`: ```ts `${url}realms/${realm}/...` ``` If someone passes `https://auth.example.com`, endpoints break. Normalize the base URL. ### `process.env.NODE_ENV` may be unsafe in browser library code This line may cause issues depending on the consumer bundler: ```ts process.env.NODE_ENV !== "production" ``` Vite apps often do not provide `process` at runtime unless replaced during build. ## Recommendation Before publishing, I’d fix at least: 1. `package.json` exports. 2. Restrict `hasRole()` client roles to the current `clientId`. 3. Decide whether roles/groups should be OR like `aya-dash` or AND as currently documented. 4. Add ID token storage/logout support or switch to `keycloak-js`. 5. Update the demo to actually use the package. 6. Document clearly: React guards are UI-only; APIs must validate JWTs server-side. If you want, I can patch the package now with the minimal safe fixes.

Those commands confirm only that TypeScript/build succeed and the tarball can be created. They **do not confirm the package is importable by consumers**.

The important red flag is still visible in your output:

```txt
CJS dist/index.js
ESM dist/index.mjs
```

But `package.json` currently points to:

```json
"main": "./dist/index.cjs",
"module": "./dist/index.js",
"exports": {
  ".": {
    "import": "./dist/index.js",
    "require": "./dist/index.cjs"
  }
}
```

So:

- `dist/index.cjs` is missing.
- `"import"` points to the CJS file.
- `"module"` points to the CJS file.
- `npm pack --dry-run` does not fail for this.

You should fix `aya-react/package.json` to:

```json
"main": "./dist/index.js",
"module": "./dist/index.mjs",
"types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
"exports": {
  ".": {
    "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
    "import": "./dist/index.mjs",
    "require": "./dist/index.js"
  }
}
```

Security/setup-wise, the biggest remaining issues are:

1. `Protected` currently uses role AND group logic, unlike `aya-dash` OR logic.
2. `hasRole()` checks resource roles from every client, not only this app’s `clientId`.
3. Tokens are in `sessionStorage`; acceptable for simple SPA use, but XSS-sensitive.
4. UI guards are not real authorization. APIs must validate JWTs server-side.
5. Logout does not use `id_token_hint`.
6. Demo does not actually exercise `AuthProvider`.

So: **PKCE is present, but the package is not fully ready yet.** The first thing I’d patch is `package.json` exports, then the role/client logic.

## About issue 5: logout / `id_token_hint`

Right now logout clears local browser storage and redirects to Keycloak logout.

That logs the app out locally, but Keycloak/OIDC logout is more complete when you send an `id_token_hint`. It tells Keycloak exactly which user session/client session is logging out.

Without it:

- Keycloak may show an extra logout confirmation screen.
- Some Keycloak versions/configs may not fully terminate the SSO session.
- User may return to the app and silently log in again because the Keycloak SSO cookie still exists.

To fix later, the library should store the `id_token` from the token response and logout with something like:

```txt
/protocol/openid-connect/logout
  ?id_token_hint=<id_token>
  &post_logout_redirect_uri=<app_origin>
```

For now, PKCE + state is good for SPA authentication if Keycloak is configured correctly.
what is id_token_hint

`id_token_hint` is an OIDC logout parameter.

It is the user’s **ID token** sent back to the identity provider during logout to say:

> “This is the session/user I want to log out.”

Example logout URL:

```txt
https://auth.example.com/realms/my-realm/protocol/openid-connect/logout
  ?id_token_hint=<ID_TOKEN>
  &post_logout_redirect_uri=https://my-app.example.com
```

Difference between tokens:

- `access_token`: used to call APIs.
- `refresh_token`: used to get new tokens.
- `id_token`: proves who the user is to the frontend/client.

`id_token_hint` uses the **ID token**, not the access token.

Why it matters:

- Helps Keycloak identify the exact browser SSO session.
- Avoids logout confirmation prompts in some setups.
- Makes logout more reliable.
- Prevents cases where your app logs out locally, but Keycloak still has an active SSO cookie and logs the user back in immediately.

So your current logout is usable, but adding `id_token_hint` makes logout more correct and complete.

For **Keycloak 26**, configure the React app as a **public OIDC client using Authorization Code + PKCE**.

## Client settings

Create/open client:

```txt
Clients → Create client
```

Use:

```txt
Client type: OpenID Connect
Client ID: your-react-client-id
Name: whatever
```

## Capability config

Set:

```txt
Client authentication: OFF
Authorization: OFF
Standard flow: ON
Direct access grants: OFF
Implicit flow: OFF
Service accounts roles: OFF
OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant: OFF
```

Important:

```txt
Proof Key for Code Exchange Code Challenge Method: S256
```

If there is a PKCE “required” option, enable it / set method to `S256`.

## Login settings

For local dev, example:

```txt
Root URL: http://localhost:5173
Home URL: http://localhost:5173
Valid redirect URIs:
  http://localhost:5173/*
Valid post logout redirect URIs:
  http://localhost:5173/*
Web origins:
  http://localhost:5173
```

For production, example:

```txt
Root URL: https://my-app.example.com
Home URL: https://my-app.example.com
Valid redirect URIs:
  https://my-app.example.com/*
Valid post logout redirect URIs:
  https://my-app.example.com/*
Web origins:
  https://my-app.example.com
```

Avoid using `*` in production except where scoped like:

```txt
https://my-app.example.com/*
```

## Required token claims

For `aya-react`, make sure the access token includes the claims you want to use:

### Realm roles

Usually already available as:

```json
realm_access.roles
```

### Client roles

Should be under:

```json
resource_access["your-react-client-id"].roles
```

### Groups

If you use groups, add a client scope / mapper:

```txt
Client scopes → create or edit scope → Add mapper → By configuration → Group Membership
```

Recommended mapper:

```txt
Token Claim Name: groups
Full group path: ON
Add to access token: ON
Add to ID token: optional
Add to userinfo: optional
```

Then assign that client scope to your React client.

## Logout config

Because we added `id_token_hint` + `post_logout_redirect_uri`, Keycloak must allow the logout redirect:

```txt
Valid post logout redirect URIs:
  https://my-app.example.com/*
```

For local:

```txt
http://localhost:5173/*
```

## Summary checklist

```txt
Client authentication OFF
Standard flow ON
Implicit flow OFF
Direct access grants OFF
PKCE S256
Valid redirect URIs configured
Valid post logout redirect URIs configured
Web origins configured
Groups mapper if using groups
Roles assigned to users/groups
APIs validate access tokens server-side
```

3. Tokens are in sessionStorage; acceptable for simple SPA use, but XSS-sensitive.
 4. UI guards are not real authorization. APIs must validate JWTs server-side.
I want to talk about these 2 issues, what is the solution?

There are two separate problems:

1. **Where browser tokens live**
2. **Where real authorization is enforced**

They have different solutions.

---

## 3. Tokens in `sessionStorage`

### Current situation

`aya-react` stores:

```txt
access_token
refresh_token
id_token
```

in `sessionStorage`.

That means tokens survive page refresh, but JavaScript can read them. If the app has an XSS bug, malicious JS can steal them.

PKCE does **not** solve XSS. PKCE protects the authorization code exchange. It does not protect tokens after the SPA receives them.

---

## Solutions for token storage

### Option A — Best security: BFF pattern

This is the strongest solution.

Instead of the React app holding tokens:

```txt
React app → your backend session → backend holds tokens
```

Flow:

1. Browser redirects to Keycloak.
2. Backend handles `/callback`.
3. Backend stores tokens server-side.
4. Browser only gets an `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, `SameSite` session cookie.
5. React calls your backend using that cookie.
6. Backend calls APIs with tokens if needed.

Benefits:

- JS cannot read tokens.
- XSS cannot steal access/refresh tokens.
- Logout/session control is much better.

Downside:

- Requires a backend/BFF.
- More infrastructure than a pure SPA.

This is what I’d recommend for sensitive internal apps.

---

### Option B — Good SPA compromise: memory-only tokens

Store tokens only in React memory, not `sessionStorage`.

Benefits:

- XSS still can abuse the current session while running.
- But token theft is harder because tokens disappear on refresh/tab close.

Downside:

- Refreshing the page loses auth state.
- App must silently re-authenticate with Keycloak.
- Browser privacy settings can make silent auth unreliable.

For `aya-react`, this would mean replacing:

```ts
sessionStorage.setItem(TOKEN_KEY, ...)
```

with React state only.

---

### Option C — Current approach, hardened

If you keep `sessionStorage`, make the app harder to XSS:

- Strong Content Security Policy.
- No unsafe inline scripts.
- No `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`.
- Sanitize any user-rendered HTML.
- Keep dependencies updated.
- Use short access token lifetime.
- Use refresh token rotation / reuse detection in Keycloak.
- Avoid storing extra sensitive data in tokens.

This is acceptable for many internal SPAs, but it is not the strongest design.

---

## 4. UI guards are not real authorization

### Current situation

This is useful:

```tsx
<Protected roles={["admin"]}>
  <AdminPanel />
</Protected>
```

But it only hides UI.

A malicious user can still:

- Open DevTools.
- Modify frontend state.
- Call APIs manually with `curl`, Postman, browser console, etc.

So frontend authorization is only UX. It is not security.

---

## Real solution: APIs must validate JWTs

Every backend/API endpoint that serves protected data must validate the access token.

The API should check:

1. Token signature using Keycloak JWKS.
2. `iss` issuer.
3. `exp` expiry.
4. `aud` / `azp` depending on Keycloak setup.
5. Required role/group claims.
6. Optional: tenant/client/app-specific constraints.

Example required checks:

```txt
Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
```

Backend validates:

```txt
iss == https://auth.example.com/realms/my-realm
token is not expired
signature matches Keycloak JWKS
user has required role/group
```

Then and only then return protected data.

---

## Recommended architecture

For simple internal SPA:

```txt
React + aya-react
  ↓ Authorization: Bearer access_token
API
  ↓ validates JWT against Keycloak
Keycloak JWKS
```

For stronger security:

```txt
React
  ↓ HttpOnly session cookie
BFF/backend
  ↓ validates session / holds tokens
API/services
```

---

## My recommendation for AYA React

Keep `aya-react` as a lightweight SPA auth helper, but document clearly:

```txt
Protected is for UI visibility only.
All APIs must validate access tokens server-side.
```

Then for sensitive apps, add a separate pattern later:

```txt
aya-bff / backend integration
```

If you want the best practical next step without redesigning everything: keep `sessionStorage` for now, but make sure every API validates Keycloak JWTs and roles server-side.
but most pages aren't spa but webpages

That changes the recommendation.

If most apps are **normal web pages / multi-page apps**, not pure SPAs, then the best solution is usually:

```txt
Browser → backend/web server → Keycloak
```

not:

```txt
Browser React → Keycloak directly
```

## Best pattern for webpages

Use **server-side authentication**:

1. User visits protected page.
2. Backend redirects to Keycloak.
3. Keycloak redirects back to backend callback.
4. Backend exchanges code for tokens.
5. Backend stores tokens/session server-side.
6. Browser only gets a secure session cookie.

The browser should not store:

```txt
access_token
refresh_token
id_token
```

Instead it only has:

```txt
HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookie
```

This is safer because JavaScript cannot read `HttpOnly` cookies.

## For your case

If the page is rendered by a backend, then `aya-react` should **not be the main security layer**.

Better split:

### Backend protects pages

Example:

```txt
GET /admin
→ backend checks session
→ if not authenticated, redirect to Keycloak
→ if authenticated and authorized, return page
```

### React only improves UX

React can still use something like:

```tsx
useAuth()
<Protected />
```

but only for convenience, menus, buttons, hiding sections, etc.

It should not decide whether the user can access the page/data.

## This is basically what `aya-dash` already does

`aya-dash` is backend-mediated:

```txt
Dash/Flask server handles auth
Flask session stores auth state
server validates access token
server protects routes
```

That is stronger than a pure frontend React auth package.

## So what should `aya-react` be?

Depends on the target apps.

### If React apps are true SPAs

Keep current `aya-react` with PKCE.

```txt
React handles login
React stores tokens
APIs validate JWTs
```

### If React is used inside normal webpages

Then `aya-react` should probably be lighter:

```txt
backend handles login/session
React receives current user from backend
React does not own tokens
```

For example the backend could expose:

```txt
GET /auth/me
```

returning:

```json
{
  "name": "João",
  "email": "joao@example.com",
  "roles": ["admin"],
  "groups": ["/finance"]
}
```

Then React uses that to render UI, but the backend still protects the real routes/actions.

## My recommendation

If most consumers are webpages, not SPAs:

1. Use backend auth as the real security layer.
2. Do not store refresh tokens in browser.
3. Keep `aya-react` only for SPA cases.
4. Consider a second React mode later:

```tsx
<AuthProvider mode="session" userEndpoint="/auth/me">
  <App />
</AuthProvider>
```

Where React reads the authenticated user from the backend instead of doing Keycloak PKCE itself.
what aya-dash does?

`aya-dash` does **server-side auth** through Flask middleware.

The browser does **not** directly manage tokens like `aya-react` does.

## `aya-dash` flow

1. User opens a Dash page.
2. Flask `before_request` checks if there is a valid session.
3. If not authenticated, Flask redirects user to Keycloak `/auth`.
4. Keycloak redirects back to Flask `/authorized`.
5. Flask exchanges the code for tokens.
6. Flask validates the JWT.
7. Flask stores auth data in the Flask server-side/session context.
8. Future page/API requests go through Flask checks before rendering data.

So the backend is the gatekeeper.

## Main files

### `authentication.py`

This is the core.

It registers:

```txt
/login
/authorized
/logout
```

And two Flask request hooks:

```txt
expire_session
check_auth
```

### `expire_session`

Before each request, it checks if the session expiry has passed.

If expired, it clears the session.

### `check_auth`

Before each request, it checks whether the user is logged in.

If not:

- normal page requests redirect to `/login`
- Dash internal requests like `/_dash*` return `401`

### `/login`

Redirects to Keycloak.

### `/authorized`

Handles Keycloak callback.

It:

1. exchanges auth code for tokens
2. validates access token signature/issuer/expiry
3. runs optional authorization validators
4. stores token/session info

### `/logout`

Clears the Flask session and redirects to Keycloak logout.

## Authorization

`aya-dash` supports validators like:

```py
validator_for_keycloak_realm_role_in(["admin"])
validator_for_keycloak_client_role_in("client-id", ["editor"])
validator_for_keycloak_group_in(["/finance"])
```

These check JWT claims server-side.

## Decorators

It also has helpers like:

```py
@require_role(...)
```

or:

```py
protect_layout(...)
protect_component(...)
protect_callback(...)
```

Those decode and verify the JWT on protected requests/callbacks.

## Security difference vs `aya-react`

### `aya-dash`

```txt
Browser → Flask/Dash server → Keycloak
```

Tokens/session are handled by Flask.

The server protects:

- routes
- callbacks
- layouts
- components

### `aya-react`

```txt
Browser React app → Keycloak
```

Tokens are handled by JavaScript in the browser.

React can hide/show UI, but it cannot enforce backend security by itself.

## In short

`aya-dash` is stronger because the backend participates in the auth flow and protects the actual resources before sending/rendering them.
doesnt react have a server?

React itself does **not** have a server.

React is a **frontend UI library**. It runs in the browser after JavaScript files are downloaded.

But React apps are often used in different hosting models:

## 1. Pure React SPA

Example: Vite, Create React App.

```txt
Browser downloads index.html + JS bundle
React runs in browser
```

There may be a static file server, but it only serves files. It does not know who the user is.

In this model:

```txt
aya-react PKCE makes sense
```

But tokens live in the browser.

## 2. React served by a backend

Example:

```txt
Flask/Django/FastAPI/Express serves page
React enhances part of the page
```

Here the backend can do auth like `aya-dash`.

```txt
Browser → backend → Keycloak
```

React does not need to login directly. It can receive user info from the backend.

## 3. React framework with a server

Examples:

- Next.js
- Remix
- React Router framework mode
- Astro with React islands

These have server-side routes/loaders/actions.

In this model, you can do stronger auth:

```txt
Browser → Next/Remix server → Keycloak
```

The server keeps tokens/session. Browser gets an HttpOnly cookie.

## So for your package

`aya-react` currently assumes model 1:

```txt
Pure SPA
React handles Keycloak directly
```

If your apps are model 2 or 3, then `aya-react` should not be the main auth system. You want backend/session auth, closer to `aya-dash`.

A good split would be:

- `aya-react`: SPA PKCE helper.
- `aya-dash`: Flask/Dash server auth.
- future maybe: `aya-session-react`: React context that reads `/auth/me` from a backend-managed session.
the thing is that i can have a fastapi backend ( or not) and frontend in react or dash, in the future I can have another kind of backend. I need to be able to interchange them

Yes — then you should separate **authentication architecture** from **UI framework**.

Right now:

- `aya-dash` = backend-managed auth for Dash/Flask.
- `aya-react` = frontend-managed PKCE auth for React SPA.

Those are different security models. If you want React, Dash, FastAPI, and future backends to be interchangeable, you need a shared contract.

## Recommended AYA architecture

Think in layers:

```txt
Keycloak / OIDC
    ↓
AYA backend adapters
    ↓
standard auth session / user contract
    ↓
AYA frontend adapters
```

## Backend adapters

Each backend integration should do the same core things:

```txt
aya-dash      → Flask/Dash middleware
aya-fastapi   → FastAPI middleware/dependencies
aya-express   → future Node/Express middleware
aya-django    → future Django middleware
```

Each backend adapter should expose the same behavior:

```txt
/login
/callback or /authorized
/logout
/auth/me
```

And enforce:

```txt
JWT validation
session expiry
roles/groups authorization
logout
```

## Frontend adapters

Frontend packages should not always authenticate directly.

React should support two modes:

---

## Mode 1: SPA mode

Current `aya-react`.

```txt
React → Keycloak directly with PKCE
React stores tokens
React calls APIs with Bearer token
APIs validate JWTs
```

Good for:

- static React apps
- no backend
- simple internal tools

API calls:

```ts
fetch("/api/data", {
  headers: {
    Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`,
  },
});
```

But every API must validate the token.

---

## Mode 2: Session mode

For apps with a backend.

```txt
React → backend session
backend → Keycloak
```

React does not store tokens.

React calls:

```txt
GET /auth/me
```

Backend returns:

```json
{
  "authenticated": true,
  "user": {
    "name": "João",
    "email": "joao@example.com",
    "username": "jbarbosa"
  },
  "roles": ["admin"],
  "groups": ["/finance"]
}
```

Then React uses that for UI:

```tsx
<AuthProvider mode="session" userEndpoint="/auth/me">
  <App />
</AuthProvider>
```

Good for:

- React served with FastAPI
- React served with Flask
- React inside a normal web app
- stronger security
- no tokens in browser JS

---

## The interchangeable contract

Define a common AYA user object:

```ts
interface AyaUser {
  name: string;
  email: string;
  username: string;
  roles: string[];
  groups: string[];
}
```

And expose the same frontend API everywhere:

```ts
const { user, authenticated, logout, hasRole, hasGroup } = useAuth();
```

Then the app code does not care whether auth came from:

```txt
Dash/Flask session
FastAPI session
React PKCE
future backend
```

## What I would build

### Keep `aya-react` but add modes later

```tsx
<AuthProvider
  mode="spa"
  url="https://auth.example.com/"
  realm="my-realm"
  clientId="my-client"
>
  <App />
</AuthProvider>
```

and:

```tsx
<AuthProvider
  mode="session"
  userEndpoint="/auth/me"
  logoutEndpoint="/logout"
>
  <App />
</AuthProvider>
```

### Build `aya-fastapi`

FastAPI should provide:

```txt
/login
/authorized
/logout
/auth/me
```

plus dependencies like:

```py
require_auth()
require_role(["admin"])
require_group(["/finance"])
```

Then React can use session mode with FastAPI.

## Best long-term rule

Use this decision tree:

```txt
No backend?
  → React SPA PKCE mode

Backend exists?
  → backend owns auth/session
  → React/Dash only consume user context

APIs exist?
  → APIs must validate JWT or session regardless of frontend
```

That gives you interchangeability without weakening security.

arguments.content:
# AYA Authentication Interchangeability Plan

## Goal

Make AYA support interchangeable frontends and backends while keeping authentication and authorization consistent across:

- React frontends
- Dash frontends
- FastAPI backends
- Flask/Dash backends
- Future backend frameworks

The main design principle is to separate **authentication enforcement** from **UI rendering**.

---

## Current State

### `aya-dash`

`aya-dash` uses a backend-managed authentication model:

```txt
Browser → Flask/Dash server → Keycloak
```

The server:

- redirects users to Keycloak
- handles the authorization callback
- exchanges the authorization code for tokens
- validates JWTs
- stores auth state in the server/session layer
- protects Dash routes, callbacks, layouts, and components

This is the stronger model because protected resources are controlled server-side.

### `aya-react`

`aya-react` currently uses a SPA authentication model:

```txt
Browser React app → Keycloak
```

The browser:

- redirects directly to Keycloak
- exchanges the authorization code using PKCE
- stores tokens in browser storage
- uses frontend role/group checks for UI visibility

This is appropriate for static React SPAs or apps without a backend, but it is not the same security model as `aya-dash`.

---

## Target Architecture

AYA should be organized around a shared contract:

```txt
Keycloak / OIDC
    ↓
AYA backend adapters
    ↓
standard auth session / user contract
    ↓
AYA frontend adapters
```

Backend integrations should enforce security.
Frontend integrations should consume auth state and provide ergonomic UI helpers.

---

## Backend Adapter Contract

Each backend adapter should expose equivalent behavior:

```txt
/login
/authorized or /callback
/logout
/auth/me
```

Each backend adapter should be responsible for:

- OIDC authorization redirect
- authorization code exchange
- JWT signature validation
- issuer validation
- expiry validation
- optional audience / client validation
- role and group authorization
- session expiry
- logout

Potential adapters:

```txt
aya-dash      → Flask/Dash middleware
aya-fastapi   → FastAPI middleware/dependencies
aya-express   → future Node/Express middleware
aya-django    → future Django middleware
```

---

## Shared User Contract

All adapters should expose a consistent user shape.

Example TypeScript contract:

```ts
interface AyaUser {
  name: string;
  email: string;
  username: string;
  roles: string[];
  groups: string[];
}
```

Example `/auth/me` response:

```json
{
  "authenticated": true,
  "user": {
    "name": "João Barbosa",
    "email": "joao@example.com",
    "username": "jbarbosa"
  },
  "roles": ["admin"],
  "groups": ["/finance"]
}
```

The exact contract should be defined once and reused by all frontend/backend packages.

---

## React Authentication Modes

`aya-react` should eventually support two modes.

### Mode 1: SPA Mode

Used when there is no backend.

```txt
React → Keycloak directly with PKCE
React stores tokens
React calls APIs with Bearer token
APIs validate JWTs
```

Example:

```tsx
<AuthProvider
  mode="spa"
  url="https://auth.example.com/"
  realm="my-realm"
  clientId="my-client"
>
  <App />
</AuthProvider>
```

Good for:

- static React apps
- simple internal tools
- apps without a backend

Important limitation:

- frontend guards are only UI helpers
- any backend/API must independently validate access tokens

### Mode 2: Session Mode

Used when a backend exists.

```txt
React → backend session
backend → Keycloak
```

React does not store access tokens, refresh tokens, or ID tokens.
It reads authenticated user state from the backend.

Example:

```tsx
<AuthProvider
  mode="session"
  userEndpoint="/auth/me"
  logoutEndpoint="/logout"
>
  <App />
</AuthProvider>
```

Good for:

- React served by FastAPI
- React served by Flask
- React embedded in server-rendered pages
- stronger security requirements

In this mode, the backend owns login/logout/session enforcement.
React only consumes user state for rendering.

---

## Frontend API Goal

Application code should use the same React API regardless of auth mode:

```ts
const { user, authenticated, logout, hasRole, hasGroup } = useAuth();
```

Components should remain mode-agnostic:

```tsx
<Protected roles={["admin"]}>
  <AdminPanel />
</Protected>
```

Whether the auth state came from browser PKCE or backend session should be an implementation detail.

---

## Authorization Rule

Frontend authorization is only for UX.

Real authorization must happen where protected data/actions are served:

- backend route handlers
- API endpoints
- Dash callbacks
- server-side page rendering

For APIs, validate at least:

- JWT signature using Keycloak JWKS
- issuer
- expiry
- audience / authorized party where applicable
- required roles/groups

---

## Decision Tree

```txt
No backend?
  → Use aya-react SPA PKCE mode

Backend exists?
  → Backend owns auth/session
  → Frontend consumes /auth/me

APIs exist?
  → APIs must validate JWT or backend session regardless of frontend
```

---

## Implementation Roadmap

### 1. Stabilize current `aya-react` SPA mode

- Keep PKCE with S256.
- Keep state validation.
- Keep client-specific role checks.
- Keep `id_token_hint` logout support.
- Clearly document that SPA mode stores tokens in the browser.
- Clearly document that `<Protected>` is a UI helper, not backend authorization.

### 2. Define shared AYA auth contract

Create a framework-neutral contract for:

- authenticated user
- roles
- groups
- `/auth/me` response
- role/group OR semantics
- logout behavior

### 3. Implement `aya-fastapi`

FastAPI should provide:

```txt
/login
/authorized or /callback
/logout
/auth/me
```

And dependencies such as:

```py
require_auth()
require_role(["admin"])
require_group(["/finance"])
```

### 4. Add `aya-react` session mode

Add support for:

```tsx
<AuthProvider mode="session" userEndpoint="/auth/me" logoutEndpoint="/logout">
  <App />
</AuthProvider>
```

This mode should:

- call `/auth/me` on startup
- expose the same `useAuth()` API
- avoid storing tokens in browser JavaScript
- call backend logout endpoint

### 5. Align `aya-dash` with the shared contract

Expose or document a Dash equivalent of `/auth/me` where useful.
Ensure role/group semantics match the shared contract.

---

## Long-Term Outcome

AYA should let teams mix and match:

```txt
React + no backend       → aya-react SPA mode
React + FastAPI          → aya-fastapi + aya-react session mode
React + Flask            → Flask adapter + aya-react session mode
Dash                     → aya-dash
Future backend framework → new backend adapter using same contract
```

The frontend API remains consistent, while the backend determines the strongest available security model.

For the current `aya-react` release, the main missing/release-blocking items are:

## Must fix before release

### 1. CI publish job needs to build before publishing

`aya-react/.gitignore` ignores `dist/`:

```txt
dist/
```

But `package.json` publishes only:

```json
"files": ["dist"]
```

So in CI, `npm publish` may publish without built files unless `dist` exists from a build artifact.

Current root `.gitlab-ci.yml` publish job does:

```bash
cd aya-react
npm publish
```

It should run:

```bash
npm ci
npm run build
npm publish
```

before publishing.

This is the biggest release blocker.

---

### 2. Add explicit release/security notes to README

README should clearly say:

- SPA mode stores tokens in browser `sessionStorage`.
- `<Protected>` is only a UI guard.
- APIs must validate JWTs server-side.
- Keycloak client must be public + PKCE S256.
- Valid redirect URI and post-logout redirect URI must be configured.

Some of this exists, but the backend/API security warning should be explicit.

---

### 3. Keycloak 26 config docs

Add a short section for KC26:

```txt
Client authentication: OFF
Standard flow: ON
Implicit flow: OFF
Direct access grants: OFF
PKCE: S256
Valid redirect URIs: https://app.example.com/*
Valid post logout redirect URIs: https://app.example.com/*
Web origins: https://app.example.com
```

---

## Should fix soon, but not necessarily blocker

### 4. URL normalization

Current code assumes `url` ends with `/`.

This works:

```ts
url="https://auth.example.com/"
```

This breaks:

```ts
url="https://auth.example.com"
```

Should normalize internally.

---

### 5. `process.env.NODE_ENV` in browser package

This may depend on bundler behavior:

```ts
process.env.NODE_ENV !== "production"
```

Vite usually replaces it, but not every consumer setup will. Safer to avoid relying on `process` directly in a browser library.

---

### 6. No tests

Not a hard blocker if this is an internal first release, but ideally add small tests for:

- role OR group behavior
- client-specific role lookup
- package import CJS/ESM
- logout URL includes `id_token_hint`

---

## Already fixed

These are now okay:

- package exports fixed
- ESM/CJS imports validated
- PKCE implemented
- state validation present
- `id_token_hint` logout added
- role checks restricted to current `clientId`
- role/group logic now matches `aya-dash` OR behavior
- demo removed
- build/typecheck pass

## My release recommendation

Before tagging, fix the CI publish job and add the README security/KC26 notes. Then this is okay for an internal `0.1.x` release.

aya-react:build:
  stage: build
  image: node:20
  rules:
    - changes:
        - aya-react/**/*
  script:
    - cd aya-react
    - npm ci
    - npm run build
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - aya-react/dist/

aya-react:publish:
  stage: publish
  image: node:20
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_TAG =~ /^react-v/
  script:
    - cd aya-react
    - |
      echo "@ltplabs:registry=${CI_API_V4_URL}/projects/${CI_PROJECT_ID}/packages/npm/" >> .npmrc
      echo "${CI_API_V4_URL#https:}/projects/${CI_PROJECT_ID}/packages/npm/:_authToken=${CI_JOB_TOKEN}" >> .npmrc
    - npm publish

aya-dash:publish:
  stage: publish
  image: python:3.11
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_TAG =~ /^dash-v/
  script:
    - pip install uv
    - cd aya-dash
    - uv build
    - >
      uv publish
      --publish-url ${CI_API_V4_URL}/projects/${CI_PROJECT_ID}/packages/pypi
      --username gitlab-ci-token
      --password ${CI_JOB_TOKEN}

# aya-react Keycloak OIDC authentication for React apps. Internal LTPLabs package. ## Install Add to your project's `.npmrc`: ```ini @ltplabs:registry=https://git.ltplabs.net/api/v4/projects/640/packages/npm/ //git.ltplabs.net/api/v4/projects/640/packages/npm/:_authToken= ``` Then: ```bash npm install @ltplabs/aya-react ``` Get a token from GitLab → User → Preferences → Access Tokens (scope: `read_api`). ## Environment Variables Create a `.env` file in your project root (copy from `.env.example`): ```ini VITE_KEYCLOAK_URL=https://auth.example.com/ VITE_REALM=my-realm VITE_OIDC_CLIENT_ID=my-client ``` > Vite requires the `VITE_` prefix to expose variables to the browser. > The Keycloak client must be **public** (`Client authentication: OFF`) with PKCE (`S256`) enabled — this library never sends a client secret. Wire them into the provider: ```tsx ``` ## Usage ```tsx import { AuthProvider, useAuth, Protected } from "@ltplabs/aya-react"; function App() { const { user, token, logout, hasRole, hasGroup } = useAuth(); return
Hello, {user.name}
; } Access denied

}> ``` ## API ### `` | Prop | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | `url` | `string` | Keycloak server URL | | `realm` | `string` | Keycloak realm | | `clientId` | `string` | Client ID for this app | Renders `null` until Keycloak initialises. Handles token refresh automatically. ### `useAuth()` | Field | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | `user` | `{ name, email, username }` | Parsed from token claims | | `token` | `string` | Raw access token for API calls | | `logout` | `() => void` | Ends session | | `hasRole(role)` | `(string) => boolean` | Checks realm roles + resource roles for this app's `clientId` | | `hasGroup(group)` | `(string) => boolean` | Checks the `groups` claim | ### `` | Prop | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | `roles` | `string[]` | User must have at least one | | `groups` | `string[]` | User must belong to at least one | | `onDenied` | `ReactNode` | Rendered when access denied (default: `null`) | #### Examples Guard by role — renders nothing if user lacks the role: ```tsx ``` Guard by role with fallback message: ```tsx You do not have access to this page.

}> ``` Guard by group: ```tsx ``` Require role OR group, matching `aya-dash` semantics: ```tsx ``` Require one of multiple roles (any one is enough): ```tsx ``` ## Publishing Push a tag matching `react-v*` — CI publishes automatically. ```bash git tag react-v0.1.0 git push origin react-v0.1.0 ```

```

## Keycloak 26 client configuration

Configure the React app as a public OIDC client:

| Setting | Value |
|---|---|
| Client authentication | `OFF` |
| Standard flow | `ON` |
| Implicit flow | `OFF` |
| Direct access grants | `OFF` |
| Service accounts roles | `OFF` |
| PKCE / Proof Key for Code Exchange | `S256` |

Configure app URLs exactly for each environment:

```txt
Valid redirect URIs:
  https://your-app.example.com/*
Valid post logout redirect URIs:
  https://your-app.example.com/*
Web origins:
  https://your-app.example.com
```

For local Vite development:

```txt
Valid redirect URIs:
  http://localhost:5173/*
Valid post logout redirect URIs:
  http://localhost:5173/*
Web origins:
  http://localhost:5173
```

If using groups, add a Keycloak group membership mapper with:

```txt
Token claim name: groups
Add to access token: ON
Full group path: ON
```

## Security notes

This package is for browser-based SPA authentication with Authorization Code + PKCE.

- Tokens are stored in `sessionStorage`, including the refresh token and ID token.
- `sessionStorage` is vulnerable to token theft if the application has an XSS vulnerability.
- `<Protected>` and `hasRole()` / `hasGroup()` are UI helpers only.
- Backend APIs must validate access tokens server-side before returning protected data.
- APIs should validate token signature, issuer, expiry, and required roles/groups.
- Use short token lifetimes and refresh token rotation/reuse detection in Keycloak where possible.

For apps with a backend, prefer a backend-managed session model where the browser only receives an `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, `SameSite` session cookie.

## Publishing

    - implicit/direct grants off
    - PKCE S256
    - redirect/post-logout/web origins
    - groups mapper
  - Added security notes:
    - tokens are in `sessionStorage`
    - XSS risk
    - `<Protected>` is UI-only
    - APIs must validate JWTs server-side
    - backend-session model preferred when backend exists.
what do I need to do to change back to 0.1.0? beside package.json?

Besides `aya-react/package.json`, change:

## 1. `aya-react/package-lock.json`

It currently has root package version entries. Change both from `0.1.3` to `0.1.0`:

```json
{
  "name": "@ltplabs/aya-react",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  ...
  "packages": {
    "": {
      "name": "@ltplabs/aya-react",
      "version": "0.1.0"
    }
  }
}
```

Easiest:

```bash
cd aya-react
npm install --package-lock-only
```

after changing `package.json`.

## 2. README publishing example, optional

Currently:

```bash
git tag react-v0.1.0
```

This already matches `0.1.0`, so no change needed.

## 3. Git tag

Release tag should match:

```bash
git tag react-v0.1.0
git push origin react-v0.1.0
```

## 4. Registry caveat

If `@ltplabs/aya-react@0.1.0` was already published to GitLab npm registry, you usually cannot republish the same version unless the old package version is deleted from the registry first.

So:

- if `0.1.0` was never published: just publish it.
- if it already exists: delete old `0.1.0` in GitLab Package Registry or use a new version.

## 5. Rebuild

After version change:

```bash
cd aya-react
npm run typecheck
npm run build
npm pack --dry-run
```

No source code changes needed.
I changed the repository, so it restarted

If the repository was recreated/restarted and the package registry is clean, then going back to `0.1.0` is fine.

Do this in `aya-react/`:

```bash
npm version 0.1.0 --no-git-tag-version
npm install --package-lock-only
npm run typecheck
npm run build
npm pack --dry-run
```

Then release with:

```bash
git tag react-v0.1.0
git push origin react-v0.1.0
```

Main files that should reflect `0.1.0`:

```txt
aya-react/package.json
aya-react/package-lock.json
```

If this is a new GitLab project/package registry, you should not have the “version already published” issue.

# MR Message

Generates a well-structured Merge Request message by analyzing the diff between the current branch and a target branch provided as an argument.

## Argument

`$1` — the target branch to diff against (e.g. `main`, `develop`). If not provided, default to `main`.

## Workflow

Follow these steps in order:

### 1. Gather context

Run the following commands in parallel:

```bash
# Commit log between target and current branch
git log $1..HEAD --oneline

# Full diff between target branch and current branch
git diff $1...HEAD

# Current branch name
git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD
```

### 2. Analyze the diff

From the diff and commit log, identify:
- **What changed**: files touched, modules affected, added/removed/modified behaviour
- **Why it changed**: infer intent from commit messages, variable names, and surrounding code
- **Type of change**: new feature, bug fix, refactor, docs, chore, config, test, etc.

### 3. Write the MR message

Produce a message in this structure:

```
## Summary

<1-3 bullet points describing what this MR does and why>

## Changes

<Bulleted list of notable changes, grouped logically if needed>
```

**Title** (one line, under 72 characters):
- Prefix with type: `feat:`, `fix:`, `refactor:`, `chore:`, etc.
- Imperative mood: "Add X", "Fix Y", "Remove Z"

## Rules

- Focus on the **why**, not just the what
- Be concise — avoid padding or filler sentences
- Group related changes together
- If the diff is large, summarize by area/module rather than listing every file
- Do not invent behaviour not present in the diff
- Output the title first, then the body in a single fenced block so it is easy to copy
</skill>

+{
+  "name": "@ltplabs/aya-react",
+  "version": "0.1.0",
+  "description": "Keycloak OIDC authentication for React apps",
+  "main": "./dist/index.js",
+  "module": "./dist/index.mjs",
+  "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
+  "exports": {
+    ".": {
+      "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
+      "import": "./dist/index.mjs",
+      "require": "./dist/index.js"
+    }
+  },
+  "files": [
+    "dist"
+  ],
+  "scripts": {
+    "build": "tsup",
+    "dev": "tsup --watch",
+    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit"
+  },
+  "peerDependencies": {

+    "react-dom": ">=17"
+  },

+  },
+  "publishConfig": {
+    "registry": "https://git.ltplabs.net/api/v4/projects/640/packages/npm/"
+  }
+}
diff --git a/aya-react/src/AuthProvider.tsx b/aya-react/src/AuthProvider.tsx
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..3521970
--- /dev/null
+++ b/aya-react/src/AuthProvider.tsx
@@ -0,0 +1,215 @@
+import React, { createContext, useEffect, useRef, useState } from "react";
+
+export interface KeycloakSettings {
+  url: string;
+  realm: string;
+  clientId: string;
+}
+
+export interface AuthContextValue {
+  token: string;
+  tokenParsed: Record<string, unknown>;
+  clientId: string;
+  logout: () => void;
+  skipAuth?: boolean;
+}
+
+export const AuthContext = createContext<AuthContextValue | null>(null);
+
+type AuthProviderProps = KeycloakSettings & { children: React.ReactNode } & (
+    | {
+        /**
+         * Dev-only bypass — skips the Keycloak redirect/token flow entirely and renders
+         * children immediately with `mockUser` as the parsed claims. Only takes effect
+         * when `process.env.NODE_ENV !== "production"`; statically dead-code-eliminated
+         * from production bundles, so it cannot be enabled by a stray env var in prod.
+         */
+        skipAuth: true;
+        mockUser: Record<string, unknown>;
+      }
+    | { skipAuth?: false; mockUser?: undefined }
+  );
+
+const TOKEN_KEY = "auth_token";
+const ID_TOKEN_KEY = "auth_id_token";
+const REFRESH_KEY = "auth_refresh_token";
+const VERIFIER_KEY = "auth_pkce_verifier";
+const STATE_KEY = "auth_state";
+
+function base64UrlEncode(bytes: Uint8Array): string {
+  let binary = "";
+  for (const byte of bytes) binary += String.fromCharCode(byte);
+  return btoa(binary).replace(/\+/g, "-").replace(/\//g, "_").replace(/=+$/, "");
+}
+
+function generateRandomString(): string {
+  return base64UrlEncode(crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(32)));
+}
+
+async function generateCodeChallenge(verifier: string): Promise<string> {
+  const digest = await crypto.subtle.digest("SHA-256", new TextEncoder().encode(verifier));
+  return base64UrlEncode(new Uint8Array(digest));
+}
+
+async function buildAuthUrl(url: string, realm: string, clientId: string): Promise<string> {
+  const verifier = generateRandomString();
+  const state = generateRandomString();
+  const challenge = await generateCodeChallenge(verifier);
+  sessionStorage.setItem(VERIFIER_KEY, verifier);
+  sessionStorage.setItem(STATE_KEY, state);
+
+  const base = `${url}realms/${realm}/protocol/openid-connect/auth`;
+  const params = new URLSearchParams({
+    client_id: clientId,
+    redirect_uri: window.location.origin,
+    response_type: "code",
+    scope: "openid",
+    state,
+    code_challenge: challenge,
+    code_challenge_method: "S256",
+  });
+  return `${base}?${params}`;
+}
+
+async function fetchTokens(
+  url: string,
+  realm: string,
+  clientId: string,
+  body: Record<string, string>
+): Promise<{ access_token: string; refresh_token: string; expires_in: number; id_token?: string }> {
+  const res = await fetch(`${url}realms/${realm}/protocol/openid-connect/token`, {
+    method: "POST",
+    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded" },
+    body: new URLSearchParams(body),
+  });
+  const data = await res.json();
+  if (!data.access_token) throw new Error(data.error_description ?? "Token request failed");
+  return data;
+}
+
+function parseJwt(token: string) {
+  const base64Url = token.split('.')[1]
+  const base64 = base64Url.replace(/-/g, '+').replace(/_/g, '/')
+  return JSON.parse(atob(base64))
+}
+
+
+export function AuthProvider({ url, realm, clientId, children, skipAuth, mockUser }: AuthProviderProps) {
+  const devSkipAuth = process.env.NODE_ENV !== "production" && skipAuth === true;
+
+  const [token, setToken] = useState<string | null>(sessionStorage.getItem(TOKEN_KEY));
+  const [initError, setInitError] = useState<string | null>(null);
+  const didInit = useRef(false);
+  const refreshTimer = useRef<ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> | null>(null);
+
+  function scheduleRefresh(expiresIn: number) {
+    if (refreshTimer.current) clearTimeout(refreshTimer.current);
+    const delay = Math.max((expiresIn - 30) * 1000, 0);
+    refreshTimer.current = setTimeout(() => doRefresh(), delay);
+  }
+
+  async function doRefresh() {
+    const refreshToken = sessionStorage.getItem(REFRESH_KEY);
+    if (!refreshToken) { logout(); return; }
+    try {
+      const data = await fetchTokens(url, realm, clientId, {
+        grant_type: "refresh_token",
+        refresh_token: refreshToken,
+        client_id: clientId,
+      });
+      sessionStorage.setItem(TOKEN_KEY, data.access_token);
+      sessionStorage.setItem(REFRESH_KEY, data.refresh_token);
+      if (data.id_token) sessionStorage.setItem(ID_TOKEN_KEY, data.id_token);
+      setToken(data.access_token);
+      scheduleRefresh(data.expires_in);
+    } catch {
+      logout();
+    }
+  }
+
+  function logout() {
+    if (refreshTimer.current) clearTimeout(refreshTimer.current);
+    const idToken = sessionStorage.getItem(ID_TOKEN_KEY);
+    sessionStorage.removeItem(TOKEN_KEY);
+    sessionStorage.removeItem(ID_TOKEN_KEY);
+    sessionStorage.removeItem(REFRESH_KEY);
+
+    const params = new URLSearchParams({
+      client_id: clientId,
+      post_logout_redirect_uri: window.location.origin,
+    });
+    if (idToken) params.set("id_token_hint", idToken);
+
+    window.location.href = `${url}realms/${realm}/protocol/openid-connect/logout?${params}`;
+  }
+
+  useEffect(() => {
+    if (devSkipAuth) return;
+    if (didInit.current) return;
+    didInit.current = true;
+
+    const params = new URLSearchParams(window.location.search);
+    const code = params.get("code");
+    const returnedState = params.get("state");
+
+    if (code) {
+      window.history.replaceState({}, "", "/");
+      const expectedState = sessionStorage.getItem(STATE_KEY);
+      const verifier = sessionStorage.getItem(VERIFIER_KEY);
+      sessionStorage.removeItem(STATE_KEY);
+      sessionStorage.removeItem(VERIFIER_KEY);
+
+      if (!verifier || !expectedState || returnedState !== expectedState) {
+        setInitError("Invalid or missing state/PKCE verifier");
+        return;
+      }
+
+      fetchTokens(url, realm, clientId, {
+        grant_type: "authorization_code",
+        code,
+        redirect_uri: window.location.origin,
+        client_id: clientId,
+        code_verifier: verifier,
+      })
+        .then((data) => {
+          sessionStorage.setItem(TOKEN_KEY, data.access_token);
+          sessionStorage.setItem(REFRESH_KEY, data.refresh_token);
+          if (data.id_token) sessionStorage.setItem(ID_TOKEN_KEY, data.id_token);
+          setToken(data.access_token);
+          scheduleRefresh(data.expires_in);
+        })
+        .catch((err) => setInitError(err.message));
+    } else if (token) {
+      const parsed = parseJwt(token);
+      const exp = parsed["exp"] as number;
+      const remaining = exp - Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000);
+      if (remaining > 0) scheduleRefresh(remaining);
+      else doRefresh();
+    } else {
+      buildAuthUrl(url, realm, clientId).then((authUrl) => {
+        window.location.href = authUrl;
+      });
+    }
+
+    return () => { if (refreshTimer.current) clearTimeout(refreshTimer.current); };
+  }, []);
+
+  if (devSkipAuth) {
+    return (
+      <AuthContext.Provider
+        value={{ token: "dev-skip-auth", tokenParsed: mockUser!, clientId, logout: () => {}, skipAuth: true }}
+      >
+        {children}
+      </AuthContext.Provider>
+    );
+  }
+
+  if (initError) return <div>Authentication failed: {initError}</div>;
+  if (!token) return null;
+
+  return (
+    <AuthContext.Provider value={{ token, tokenParsed: parseJwt(token), clientId, logout }}>
+      {children}
+    </AuthContext.Provider>
+  );
+}
diff --git a/aya-react/src/Protected.tsx b/aya-react/src/Protected.tsx
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..8b86ec4
--- /dev/null
+++ b/aya-react/src/Protected.tsx
@@ -0,0 +1,19 @@
+import React from "react";
+import { useAuth } from "./useAuth";
+
+interface ProtectedProps {
+  roles?: string[];
+  groups?: string[];
+  onDenied?: React.ReactNode;
+  children: React.ReactNode;
+}
+
+export function Protected({ roles = [], groups = [], onDenied = null, children }: ProtectedProps) {
+  const { hasRole, hasGroup } = useAuth();
+
+  const checks = [roles.length > 0 && roles.some(hasRole), groups.length > 0 && groups.some(hasGroup)];
+  const hasRequirements = roles.length > 0 || groups.length > 0;
+
+  if (!hasRequirements || checks.some(Boolean)) return <>{children}</>;
+  return <>{onDenied}</>;
+}

diff --git a/aya-react/src/useAuth.ts b/aya-react/src/useAuth.ts
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..8678150
--- /dev/null
+++ b/aya-react/src/useAuth.ts
@@ -0,0 +1,45 @@
+import { useContext } from "react";
+import { AuthContext } from "./AuthProvider";
+
+export interface AuthUser {
+  name: string;
+  email: string;
+  username: string;
+}
+
+export interface UseAuthResult {
+  user: AuthUser;
+  token: string;
+  logout: () => void;
+  hasRole: (role: string) => boolean;
+  hasGroup: (group: string) => boolean;
+}
+
+export function useAuth(): UseAuthResult {
+  const ctx = useContext(AuthContext);
+  if (!ctx) throw new Error("useAuth must be used inside <AuthProvider>");
+
+  const { token, tokenParsed, clientId, logout, skipAuth } = ctx;
+
+  const user: AuthUser = {
+    name: (tokenParsed["name"] as string) ?? "",
+    email: (tokenParsed["email"] as string) ?? "",
+    username: (tokenParsed["preferred_username"] as string) ?? "",
+  };
+
+  function hasRole(role: string): boolean {
+    if (skipAuth) return true;
+    const realmRoles = (tokenParsed["realm_access"] as { roles?: string[] } | undefined)?.roles ?? [];
+    const resourceAccess = tokenParsed["resource_access"] as Record<string, { roles?: string[] }> | undefined;
+    const clientRoles = resourceAccess?.[clientId]?.roles ?? [];
+    return realmRoles.includes(role) || clientRoles.includes(role);
+  }
+
+  function hasGroup(group: string): boolean {
+    if (skipAuth) return true;
+    const groups = (tokenParsed["groups"] as string[] | undefined) ?? [];
+    return groups.includes(group);
+  }
+
+  return { user, token, logout, hasRole, hasGroup };
+}

+{
+  "name": "@ltplabs/aya-react",
+  "version": "0.1.0",
+  "description": "Keycloak OIDC authentication for React apps",
+  "main": "./dist/index.js",
+  "module": "./dist/index.mjs",
+  "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
+  "exports": {
+    ".": {
+      "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
+      "import": "./dist/index.mjs",
+      "require": "./dist/index.js"
+    }
+  },
+  "files": [
+    "dist"
+  ],
+  "scripts": {
+    "build": "tsup",
+    "dev": "tsup --watch",
+    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit"

+  "publishConfig": {
+    "registry": "https://git.ltplabs.net/api/v4/projects/640/packages/npm/"
+  }
+}
diff --git a/aya-react/src/AuthProvider.tsx b/aya-react/src/AuthProvider.tsx
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..3521970
--- /dev/null
+++ b/aya-react/src/AuthProvider.tsx
@@ -0,0 +1,215 @@
+import React, { createContext, useEffect, useRef, useState } from "react";
+
+export interface KeycloakSettings {
+  url: string;
+  realm: string;
+  clientId: string;
+}
+
+export interface AuthContextValue {
+  token: string;
+  tokenParsed: Record<string, unknown>;
+  clientId: string;
+  logout: () => void;
+  skipAuth?: boolean;
+}
+
+export const AuthContext = createContext<AuthContextValue | null>(null);
+
+type AuthProviderProps = KeycloakSettings & { children: React.ReactNode } & (
+    | {
+        /**
+         * Dev-only bypass — skips the Keycloak redirect/token flow entirely and renders
+         * children immediately with `mockUser` as the parsed claims. Only takes effect
+         * when `process.env.NODE_ENV !== "production"`; statically dead-code-eliminated
+         * from production bundles, so it cannot be enabled by a stray env var in prod.
+         */
+        skipAuth: true;
+        mockUser: Record<string, unknown>;
+      }
+    | { skipAuth?: false; mockUser?: undefined }
+  );
+
+const TOKEN_KEY = "auth_token";
+const ID_TOKEN_KEY = "auth_id_token";
+const REFRESH_KEY = "auth_refresh_token";
+const VERIFIER_KEY = "auth_pkce_verifier";
+const STATE_KEY = "auth_state";
+
+function base64UrlEncode(bytes: Uint8Array): string {
+  let binary = "";
+  for (const byte of bytes) binary += String.fromCharCode(byte);
+  return btoa(binary).replace(/\+/g, "-").replace(/\//g, "_").replace(/=+$/, "");
+}
+
+function generateRandomString(): string {
+  return base64UrlEncode(crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(32)));
+}
+
+async function generateCodeChallenge(verifier: string): Promise<string> {
+  const digest = await crypto.subtle.digest("SHA-256", new TextEncoder().encode(verifier));
+  return base64UrlEncode(new Uint8Array(digest));
+}
+
+async function buildAuthUrl(url: string, realm: string, clientId: string): Promise<string> {
+  const verifier = generateRandomString();
+  const state = generateRandomString();
+  const challenge = await generateCodeChallenge(verifier);
+  sessionStorage.setItem(VERIFIER_KEY, verifier);
+  sessionStorage.setItem(STATE_KEY, state);
+
+  const base = `${url}realms/${realm}/protocol/openid-connect/auth`;
+  const params = new URLSearchParams({
+    client_id: clientId,
+    redirect_uri: window.location.origin,
+    response_type: "code",
+    scope: "openid",
+    state,
+    code_challenge: challenge,
+    code_challenge_method: "S256",
+  });
+  return `${base}?${params}`;
+}
+
+async function fetchTokens(
+  url: string,
+  realm: string,
+  clientId: string,
+  body: Record<string, string>
+): Promise<{ access_token: string; refresh_token: string; expires_in: number; id_token?: string }> {
+  const res = await fetch(`${url}realms/${realm}/protocol/openid-connect/token`, {
+    method: "POST",
+    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded" },
+    body: new URLSearchParams(body),
+  });
+  const data = await res.json();
+  if (!data.access_token) throw new Error(data.error_description ?? "Token request failed");
+  return data;
+}
+
+function parseJwt(token: string) {
+  const base64Url = token.split('.')[1]
+  const base64 = base64Url.replace(/-/g, '+').replace(/_/g, '/')
+  return JSON.parse(atob(base64))
+}
+
+
+export function AuthProvider({ url, realm, clientId, children, skipAuth, mockUser }: AuthProviderProps) {
+  const devSkipAuth = process.env.NODE_ENV !== "production" && skipAuth === true;
+
+  const [token, setToken] = useState<string | null>(sessionStorage.getItem(TOKEN_KEY));
+  const [initError, setInitError] = useState<string | null>(null);
+  const didInit = useRef(false);
+  const refreshTimer = useRef<ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> | null>(null);
+
+  function scheduleRefresh(expiresIn: number) {
+    if (refreshTimer.current) clearTimeout(refreshTimer.current);
+    const delay = Math.max((expiresIn - 30) * 1000, 0);
+    refreshTimer.current = setTimeout(() => doRefresh(), delay);
+  }
+
+  async function doRefresh() {
+    const refreshToken = sessionStorage.getItem(REFRESH_KEY);
+    if (!refreshToken) { logout(); return; }
+    try {
+      const data = await fetchTokens(url, realm, clientId, {
+        grant_type: "refresh_token",
+        refresh_token: refreshToken,
+        client_id: clientId,
+      });
+      sessionStorage.setItem(TOKEN_KEY, data.access_token);
+      sessionStorage.setItem(REFRESH_KEY, data.refresh_token);
+      if (data.id_token) sessionStorage.setItem(ID_TOKEN_KEY, data.id_token);
+      setToken(data.access_token);
+      scheduleRefresh(data.expires_in);
+    } catch {
+      logout();
+    }
+  }
+
+  function logout() {
+    if (refreshTimer.current) clearTimeout(refreshTimer.current);
+    const idToken = sessionStorage.getItem(ID_TOKEN_KEY);
+    sessionStorage.removeItem(TOKEN_KEY);
+    sessionStorage.removeItem(ID_TOKEN_KEY);
+    sessionStorage.removeItem(REFRESH_KEY);
+
+    const params = new URLSearchParams({
+      client_id: clientId,
+      post_logout_redirect_uri: window.location.origin,
+    });
+    if (idToken) params.set("id_token_hint", idToken);
+
+    window.location.href = `${url}realms/${realm}/protocol/openid-connect/logout?${params}`;
+  }
+
+  useEffect(() => {
+    if (devSkipAuth) return;
+    if (didInit.current) return;
+    didInit.current = true;
+
+    const params = new URLSearchParams(window.location.search);
+    const code = params.get("code");
+    const returnedState = params.get("state");
+
+    if (code) {
+      window.history.replaceState({}, "", "/");
+      const expectedState = sessionStorage.getItem(STATE_KEY);
+      const verifier = sessionStorage.getItem(VERIFIER_KEY);
+      sessionStorage.removeItem(STATE_KEY);
+      sessionStorage.removeItem(VERIFIER_KEY);
+
+      if (!verifier || !expectedState || returnedState !== expectedState) {
+        setInitError("Invalid or missing state/PKCE verifier");
+        return;
+      }
+
+      fetchTokens(url, realm, clientId, {
+        grant_type: "authorization_code",
+        code,
+        redirect_uri: window.location.origin,
+        client_id: clientId,
+        code_verifier: verifier,
+      })
+        .then((data) => {
+          sessionStorage.setItem(TOKEN_KEY, data.access_token);
+          sessionStorage.setItem(REFRESH_KEY, data.refresh_token);
+          if (data.id_token) sessionStorage.setItem(ID_TOKEN_KEY, data.id_token);
+          setToken(data.access_token);
+          scheduleRefresh(data.expires_in);
+        })
+        .catch((err) => setInitError(err.message));
+    } else if (token) {
+      const parsed = parseJwt(token);
+      const exp = parsed["exp"] as number;
+      const remaining = exp - Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000);
+      if (remaining > 0) scheduleRefresh(remaining);
+      else doRefresh();
+    } else {
+      buildAuthUrl(url, realm, clientId).then((authUrl) => {
+        window.location.href = authUrl;
+      });
+    }
+
+    return () => { if (refreshTimer.current) clearTimeout(refreshTimer.current); };
+  }, []);
+
+  if (devSkipAuth) {
+    return (
+      <AuthContext.Provider
+        value={{ token: "dev-skip-auth", tokenParsed: mockUser!, clientId, logout: () => {}, skipAuth: true }}
+      >
+        {children}
+      </AuthContext.Provider>
+    );
+  }
+
+  if (initError) return <div>Authentication failed: {initError}</div>;
+  if (!token) return null;
+
+  return (
+    <AuthContext.Provider value={{ token, tokenParsed: parseJwt(token), clientId, logout }}>
+      {children}
+    </AuthContext.Provider>
+  );
+}
diff --git a/aya-react/src/Protected.tsx b/aya-react/src/Protected.tsx
new file mode 100644

--- /dev/null
+++ b/aya-react/src/Protected.tsx
@@ -0,0 +1,19 @@
+import React from "react";
+import { useAuth } from "./useAuth";
+
+interface ProtectedProps {
+  roles?: string[];
+  groups?: string[];
+  onDenied?: React.ReactNode;
+  children: React.ReactNode;
+}
+
+export function Protected({ roles = [], groups = [], onDenied = null, children }: ProtectedProps) {
+  const { hasRole, hasGroup } = useAuth();
+
+  const checks = [roles.length > 0 && roles.some(hasRole), groups.length > 0 && groups.some(hasGroup)];
+  const hasRequirements = roles.length > 0 || groups.length > 0;
+
+  if (!hasRequirements || checks.some(Boolean)) return <>{children}</>;
+  return <>{onDenied}</>;
+}
